summary:
The Sintashta culture was a Bronze Age archaeological culture of the Eurasian steppe, centered in the southern Ural region of modern Russia and Kazakhstan, dating to approximately 2100–1800 BC. It is named after the Sintashta archaeological site in Chelyabinsk Oblast. Sintashta settlements were small, heavily fortified towns with distinctive circular or rectangular plans, built from timber and earthen walls, and located along rivers in the steppe zone. These so-called "country of towns" sites, of which over twenty have been identified, represent some of the earliest known planned urban settlements on the Eurasian steppe.

The Sintashta culture is of exceptional historical importance for two reasons. First, it provides the earliest archaeological evidence for the [[event:1859|spoked wheel and chariot]], found in Sintashta burial mounds alongside horse bones and chariot fittings, indicating that this culture may have been the originator or among the earliest adopters of this transformative technology. Second, Sintashta is closely associated by linguists and geneticists with the early Indo-Iranian branch of the Indo-European language family, making it a probable ancestral culture of the [[event:1849|Indo-Aryan]] peoples who later migrated into South Asia and the Near East.

Sintashta burials are rich in bronze weapons, horse sacrifices, and evidence of sophisticated metallurgical production, indicating a warrior elite with strong ideological associations with horses and combat. The culture's metallurgical output was extensive, supplying bronze objects across a wide steppe network. Sintashta overlaps chronologically and culturally with the closely related [[event:1861|Andronovo culture]] that succeeded or co-existed with it across the broader steppe zone.
